policy_statement: Enable resource-efficient use of the built environment by making it easier for builders to adapt existing buildings instead of demolition and new production (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: Making it easier to adapt and reuse existing buildings instead of demolishing and building new ones reduces new material production and construction activity, lowering embodied greenhouse gas emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: adapt existing buildings instead of demolition and new production
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (floor area/utility) → ↓ new materials + ↓ demolition/new build activity → ↓ embodied GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Planning, permitting, zoning, and development practices are shifted to favor renovating, retrofitting, and repurposing existing structures instead of demolishing and constructing new buildings.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced extraction, manufacturing, and transport of construction materials (e.g., cement, steel, glass) and reduced onsite construction processes, which together lower upstream and onsite energy use and process emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower lifecycle/embodied GHG emissions associated with the built environment (often substantial for concrete and steel), thereby reducing overall emissions attributable to building development.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding embodied greenhouse gas emissions by reducing demand for new construction materials and construction activity through reusing existing buildings.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Delivering comparable building function while avoiding new material production and new-build construction leads to lower embodied emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the outcome is GHG mitigation, the mechanism achieves it chiefly through reducing material/energy inputs per unit of service (efficiency/circularity), which is the defining feature of Resource Efficiency in this taxonomy.  
3) Edge cases applied: If adaptive reuse is pursued mainly for hazard resilience (e.g., floodproofing) it would shift toward Adaptation, but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism; here the stated and typical dominant effect is embodied-emissions avoidance.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that facilitates adaptive reuse instead of demolition/new construction will, by design, reduce demand for new materials and associated embodied emissions; therefore Resource Efficiency is consistently primary for this mechanism.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtype (e.g., mitigation co-benefit magnitude, operational energy impacts, affordability/heritage co-benefits) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some adaptive-reuse projects can increase operational energy use (e.g., constraints on envelope upgrades) or include major structural additions that reduce embodied savings; however, the dominant, mechanism-defining impact remains avoided new-build embodied emissions. If a policy’s principal intent is resilience upgrades rather than avoiding new construction, reclassification at the policy level may be warranted.
key_indicators: “Enable resource-efficient use of the built environment”; “making it easier for builders to adapt existing buildings”; “instead of demolition and new production”
co_benefits: Reduced construction waste/landfill; lower local air pollution and noise from demolition/construction; potential cost savings; preservation of cultural/heritage assets
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could also be implemented via specific regulatory mandates (e.g., tighter demolition permits) or incentives (e.g., reduced fees), but the text does not specify the tool—only the intent to lower barriers—so it is classified as a planning/strategy enabling measure.
